name: Apples

book_claims.claim: Lower glycaemic impact; should be eaten whole not juiced
book_claims.mechanism: Whole fruit food matrix and fibre slow sugar digestion; juicing destroys matrix and releases free sugars for rapid absorption

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis (Aune et al., 2017, BMJ) found that each daily serving of fruit (including apples) was associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and all-cause mortality, with up to 10% reduction per serving.
Flavonoid-rich apple consumption improved endothelial function and reduced LDL oxidation in hypercholesterolemic adults (Bondonno et al., 2012).
An apple a day was associated with up to 40% reduced risk of cardiovascular mortality in a meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies (Dreher, 2020).

culinary_pairings.source: Classic apple pie and baked apple pairing
Prevents oxidation; brightens apple flavour in salads and sauces
Baked apples with honey; Rosh Hashanah tradition

seasonality: Peak harvest August-November in Northern Hemisphere. Many varieties store well through winter. Available year-round but flavour peaks in autumn.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Fat-soluble; absorption improved when eaten with healthy fats. Apple skin contains 2-6x more quercetin than flesh.
Fermented by gut bacteria into short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate) that feed colonocytes and reduce inflammation.
Modest amounts; varies by variety. Contributes to total daily intake when eaten regularly.
Chlorogenic acid well-absorbed in small intestine; catechins partially absorbed. Most concentrated in skin.
Mix of soluble (pectin) and insoluble fibre. Whole apple provides intact food matrix that slows glucose absorption.
